title: Cornbread
subtitle: American Southern quick bread, crisp-edged and golden.
excerpt: A simple quick bread of polenta (cornmeal), plain flour, buttermilk and eggs, baked in a hot cast-iron skillet. Crisp on the bottom, tender in the middle. Serves six alongside chilli, soup or pulled pork.
sourceNotes: American Southern cornbread. Cornbread has been a staple of the American South since the colonial period, made by Native Americans and adopted widely. Mary Randolph's The Virginia Housewife (1824, public domain) contains early versions. Southern cornbread traditionally has no sugar; Northern and modern versions often include it. This recipe is unsweetened.

content.text: The cast-iron skillet is not optional. Preheating it with oil in the oven creates the defining feature of Southern cornbread: a shatteringly crisp, dark base with a golden, tender crumb above. A cake tin produces a pale, stodgy result by comparison. If you do not have a cast-iron skillet, a heavy ovenproof frying pan or a square baking tin will get closer than an ordinary round cake tin.

items.name: Buttermilk
items.prepNote: or plain yoghurt thinned with milk

content.text: Place the cast-iron skillet in the oven. Heat the oven to 220°C and let the skillet heat for at least 15 minutes. This is what makes the crust.

Combine {{polenta}} of fine polenta, {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{baking-powder}} of baking powder and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a large bowl. In a separate bowl, whisk {{eggs}} eggs with {{buttermilk}} of buttermilk and {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter. Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and stir until just combined; a few lumps are fine.

Carefully remove the hot skillet from the oven. Add {{rapeseed-oil}} of oil; swirl to coat. Pour the batter in immediately; it should sizzle on contact. Bake for 20 to 22 minutes until golden on top and a skewer inserted at the centre comes out clean. Leave to rest for 5 minutes, then cut into wedges.

items.fix: The sizzle when the batter hits the pan is the sign that the crust will form. If there is no sizzle, the skillet was not hot enough.
items.cause: Skillet not hot enough before the batter went in.

items.fix: Use fine polenta (cornmeal). Coarse polenta gives a gritty result. The ratio of 150 g polenta to 100 g flour is deliberate; increase the flour share if you want a softer crumb.
items.cause: Too much polenta, or coarse-ground instead of fine.

items.fix: Use a 23 to 25 cm skillet. In a larger pan the batter spreads too thin and the centre sets before the edges colour.
items.cause: Oven running cool, or skillet too large for the batter volume.

content.text: Cornbread is the bread of the American South: served with chilli, beans, pulled pork, or a pot of soup. It is eaten on the side rather than before, and it is there to mop. The Southern baker's rule is that it has no sugar; the Northern baker's is that it can. Both produce a good result; this recipe sides with the South.
